title: Primary Hyperparathyroidism
chief_complaint: I've had kidney stones twice in the past year. My bones ache. I'm tired, depressed, and constipated. I feel like I'm falling apart. My doctor says my calcium is high.

physical_exam.Neck: No palpable thyroid nodules or parathyroid mass.
physical_exam.Musculoskeletal: Mild proximal muscle weakness. No bone tenderness.

hidden_data.cmp: Calcium 11.8 mg/dL (hypercalcemia), Phosphorus 2.2 (low), ALP 180 (elevated), Albumin 4.0, Creatinine 1.2
hidden_data.pth: Intact PTH 280 pg/mL (normal 10-65) - INappropriately normal/elevated for hypercalcemia (should be suppressed)
hidden_data.vitamin_d: 25-OH Vitamin D 25 (low-normal), 1,25-OH Vitamin D 85 (elevated - PTH stimulates 1-alpha-hydroxylase)
hidden_data.urinary_calcium: 24h urinary calcium 420 mg/24h (elevated - not familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia)
hidden_data.us_neck: 2.5cm hypoechoic mass posterior to right thyroid lobe - consistent with parathyroid adenoma.
hidden_data.sestamibi: Tc-99m Sestamibi scan: Focal uptake in right inferior parathyroid gland. Confirms solitary adenoma.
hidden_data.dexa: Osteoporosis at distal radius (T-score -2.8). Femoral neck T-score -2.2. Lumbar spine T-score -1.8.
hidden_data.renal_us: Bilateral nephrolithiasis (3 stones right kidney, 2 stones left kidney).

correct_diagnosis: Primary Hyperparathyroidism (Solitary Parathyroid Adenoma) with Hypercalcemia, Osteoporosis, and Nephrolithiasis
key_learning_points: Primary hyperparathyroidism = excessive PTH secretion from parathyroid adenoma (85%), hyperplasia (15%), or carcinoma (<1%)
Classic: Hypercalcemia + Hypophosphatemia + Elevated/Inappropriately normal PTH. Remember: 'Bones, stones, groans, psychiatric overtones'
Surgical criteria: Calcium >1mg/dL above normal, Osteoporosis (T-score ≤-2.5), Age <50, eGFR <60, Nephrolithiasis, 24h urinary calcium >400mg. This patient meets ALL criteria
Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcemia (FHH): Similar labs but LOW 24h urinary calcium (<100mg), no end-organ damage. Does NOT require surgery

Treatment: Parathyroidectomy (minimally invasive if localized). Post-op: Hungry bone syndrome (hypocalcemia from rapid Ca uptake into bones). Monitor Ca q6-12h. Start calcium + calcitriol if symptomatic
